user_prompt: A user requested deletion of all their data. Generate the DSAR-deletion checklist including which systems to scrub.
actual_output:
# Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) — Deletion Checklist

**Disclaimer:** This checklist is compliance guidance. Consult your legal team and DPO (if designated) before executing deletions, particularly regarding retention obligations and litigation holds.

---

## Pre-Deletion Verification (Complete Before Proceeding)

- [ ] **Confirm requestor identity** — Verify the individual is the data subject or authorized representative (Art. 12(2))
- [ ] **Document request receipt** — Log date, method, and content of deletion request
- [ ] **Check for legal holds** — Confirm no litigation, regulatory investigation, or contractual retention obligation applies
- [ ] **Assess lawful basis for retention** — Identify any legitimate reason to retain data (e.g., tax law, contract performance, legal claim defense)
- [ ] **Determine scope** — Clarify whether deletion applies to all data or specific categories (e.g., "delete my account but keep transaction history for billing")

---

## System-by-System Deletion Checklist

### **1. Primary Application Database**
- [ ] Identify all tables/collections containing requestor's personal data
- [ ] Execute deletion query (soft delete with timestamp OR hard delete per retention policy)
- [ ] Verify deletion in production environment
- [ ] Document deletion timestamp and method (query/script used)
- [ ] **Retention note:** If soft-deleted, confirm hard deletion timeline (e.g., 30 days post-request)

### **2. Backup & Archive Systems**
- [ ] Identify backup retention schedule (daily, weekly, monthly snapshots)
- [ ] **Action:** Data will be automatically purged when backup expires OR manually delete from archive storage
- [ ] Document backup deletion timeline (e.g., "deleted from active backups; will be purged from 90-day archive by [DATE]")
- [ ] **Note:** Inform requestor that backups may retain data temporarily — this is compliant if documented (Art. 17(3)(e))

### **3. Analytics & Logging Systems**
- [ ] **Google Analytics / Mixpanel / Amplitude:** Delete user profile and associated event history
  - [ ] Remove from audience segments
  - [ ] Purge historical event data
- [ ] **Application logs:** Search for requestor identifiers (email, user ID, IP if linked to account)
  - [ ] Delete or anonymize log entries containing personal data
  - [ ] Retain anonymized logs for security/debugging (compliant under Art. 17(3)(e))
- [ ] **Error tracking (Sentry, Rollbar, etc.):** Remove requestor data from error reports and session replays

### **4. Email & Communication Systems**
- [ ] **Email service provider (Mailchimp, SendGrid, etc.):** Delete contact record and unsubscribe
- [ ] **Customer support tickets (Zendesk, Intercom, etc.):** Anonymize or delete requestor's messages
  - [ ] Consider: Retain ticket structure for support history, but remove personal identifiers
- [ ] **Chat/messaging logs:** Delete direct messages, support chat history
- [ ] **Email archives:** Search mailbox for requestor's emails; delete or anonymize

### **5. Cloud Storage & File Systems**
- [ ] **AWS S3 / Google Cloud Storage / Azure Blob:** Search for files containing requestor's personal data
  - [ ] Delete user-uploaded files
  - [ ] Delete generated reports/exports with personal data
  - [ ] Delete profile pictures, documents, attachments
- [ ] **Shared drives (Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox):** Remove requestor's files and shared access
- [ ] **CDN caches (CloudFlare, Akamai):** Purge cached content containing personal data (if applicable)

### **6. Third-Party Processors & Integrations**
- [ ] **Payment processor (Stripe, PayPal, Square):** Submit deletion request per their DSAR process
  - [ ] Note: May retain transaction data for tax/legal compliance (Art. 17(3)(c))
- [ ] **CRM system (Salesforce, HubSpot):** Delete contact record and associated interactions
- [ ] **Marketing automation (ActiveCampaign, Klaviyo):** Delete subscriber profile and behavioral data
- [ ] **Hosting provider (AWS, Heroku, DigitalOcean):** Confirm deletion in application database covers their systems
- [ ] **Third-party APIs (Twilio, SendGrid, etc.):** Submit deletion requests to each processor
- [ ] **Data brokers / enrichment services:** If you purchased data about this user, request deletion from vendor

### **7. Search Indexes & Caches**
- [ ] **Elasticsearch / Solr / Algolia:** Delete indexed documents containing requestor's data
- [ ] **Redis / Memcached:** Purge cached user sessions and profiles
- [ ] **Search engine caches (Google, Bing):** Submit URL removal requests via Search Console (optional but recommended)

### **8. Data Warehouse & Business Intelligence**
- [ ] **Data warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift):** Delete or anonymize requestor's records
- [ ] **BI dashboards (Tableau, Looker, Power BI):** Verify deletion propagates to reports (may require refresh)
- [ ] **Historical analytics tables:** Delete or pseudonymize requestor's data

### **9. Employee/Internal Systems**
- [ ] **HR systems:** If requestor is/was employee, delete from payroll, benefits, performance records (unless legal hold applies)
- [ ] **Internal communication (Slack, Teams):** Delete direct messages and mentions (if policy allows)
- [ ] **Access control systems:** Remove user accounts and audit logs (retain anonymized access records for security)

### **10. Legal/Compliance Systems**
- [ ] **Audit logs:** Retain anonymized deletion record (who requested, when, what was deleted) for compliance proof
- [ ] **Consent management platform (OneTrust, TrustArc):** Delete consent records
- [ ] **Data inventory/mapping documents:** Update to reflect deletion

---

## Post-Deletion Verification

- [ ] **Spot-check:** Query systems to confirm data is no longer retrievable
- [ ] **Test DSAR:** Submit test access request to verify data is gone
- [ ] **Document completion:** Create deletion certificate with:
  - Requestor identifier (anonymized)
  - Systems affected
  - Deletion date and method
  - Exceptions (e.g., "transaction data retained for 7 years per tax law")
- [ ] **Notify requestor:** Send confirmation within 30 days of request (Art. 12(4))

---

## Response Template to Requestor

---

**Subject: Your Data Deletion Request — Completed**

Dear [Requestor Name],

We have processed your deletion request dated [DATE] under Article 17 (Right to Erasure) of the GDPR.

**Deleted:**
- User account and profile data
- Transaction history (except [EXCEPTION if applicable])
- Analytics and behavioral data
- Communications and support tickets (anonymized)

**Retained (Legal Basis):**
- Transaction records for 7 years (tax law compliance, Art. 17(3)(c))
- Anonymized audit logs for security purposes (Art. 17(3)(e))

**Timeline:**
- Immediate deletion: Primary database, backups will be purged by [DATE]
- Third-party processors notified: [LIST]

You may verify deletion by submitting a new data access request. If you have questions, contact [DPO/Privacy Contact].

Regards,  
[Organization] Privacy Team

---

## Risk Mitigation Notes

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|
| **Incomplete deletion** (data remains in backup) | Document backup retention schedule; inform requestor of timeline |
| **Third-party processor non-compliance** | Maintain records of deletion requests sent; follow up if no confirmation within 30 days |
| **Accidental re-collection** | Review data collection forms/flows to prevent re-adding requestor's data |
| **Litigation hold conflict** | Escalate to legal counsel; may need to retain data despite deletion request (Art. 17(3)(d)) |

---

**Next Step:** After deletion, update your Data Processing Register to reflect the deletion and any exceptions. This demonstrates accountability under Article 5(2).
judge_reasoning: The chatbot provided a comprehensive and detailed DSAR deletion checklist and system-by-system guide as requested, fully adhering to its persona as a GDPR Auditor.
